title: Salad-e shirazi
subtitle: Persian finely diced cucumber, tomato, onion, and mint with lime.
excerpt: The Persian everyday salad. Cucumber, tomato, and onion all diced into the same fine cubes; dressed lightly with lime juice, olive oil, dried mint, and salt. Bright, sharp, fresh; the standard partner to any kebab, stew, or rice dish at a Persian table. Ten minutes; serves four.
sourceNotes:
Persian home cookery from Shiraz in southern Iran. Documented in Margaret Shaida's The Legendary Cuisine of Persia and in twentieth-century Iranian cookbooks. The salad sits on the everyday Persian table next to kebabs, rice dishes, and stews; it is the workaday counterpoint to the richness of polos and khoreshes.

The defining detail is the dice: cucumber, tomato, and onion all in matching fine cubes around 5 mm. Rough chunks make it a different salad.

Lime juice rather than vinegar is canonical. Lemon stands in if lime is not to hand.

content.text: Salad-e shirazi is the Persian everyday salad: cucumber, tomato, and onion all diced into the same fine cubes, dressed with
juice, olive oil, dried mint, and a pinch of salt. Bright, sharp, fresh, and the standard partner to any Iranian rice plate, kebab, or stew.
The dice is the dish. Cucumber, tomato, and onion in matching fine cubes, around 5 mm. The same knife work that goes into a Lebanese tabbouleh; rough chunks turn this into a generic chopped salad.

Lime is canonical, not vinegar. Lemon stands in if limes are not to hand, and is the usual switch outside Iran. Olive oil is the universal modern dressing; classic Iranian kitchens sometimes use no oil at all and let the cucumber juice carry it.

items.name: Cucumber
items.prepNote: small Persian or Lebanese cucumbers if you can find them

items.name: Lime
items.prepNote: juice only, about 2 tbsp; lemon if no lime

items.name: Mint
items.prepNote: dried, crumbled, or 1 tbsp fresh chopped

content.text: Halve {{tomato}} of tomatoes through the equator. Scoop out the seeds and watery pulp with the tip of a teaspoon, working over a bowl. Discard the seed pulp; the tomato flesh stays firm without the wet centre.

Cut {{cucumber}} cucumbers lengthways in quarters, then chop into 5 mm cubes. Do the same with the deseeded tomato flesh. Halve and dice {{red-onion}} red onion to the same size. Tip everything into the salad bowl. The pieces should be similar enough in size that they marry on the spoon.

Squeeze the juice of {{lime}} lime over the salad. Add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and {{mint}} of dried mint, crumbled between your fingers. Salt and pepper. Toss gently with a wooden spoon. Leave for five minutes before serving so the mint hydrates and the salt brings out the vegetable juices. Taste; adjust acid or salt.

attrs.intro: It is a forgiving salad except for the cucumber and the timing.

items.fix: Scoop the seeds out before dicing. Dress at the table, not in the kitchen; held more than thirty minutes the salad becomes a slurry.
items.cause: Tomato not deseeded, or salad held too long after dressing.

items.fix: Persian or Lebanese cucumbers (small, firm, thin-skinned) carry less water and more flavour. If using a larger English cucumber, peel and deseed it first; the salad turns out cleaner.
items.cause: Standard supermarket cucumber, peeled or unpeeled.

items.fix: Dice the onion as fine as you can; it should be barely visible in the bowl. A milder white onion or a couple of finely chopped spring onion bulbs work if a strong red onion is all you have.
items.cause: Onion dice too coarse, or red onion stale.

items.fix: Persian salads carry a generous pinch of salt; the tomato and cucumber both call for more than you might expect. Salt at dressing time and taste before serving.
items.cause: Salt added too late or in too little.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Some Iranian cooks fold in a handful of finely diced green pepper for crunch. A small spoon of finely chopped flat-leaf parsley turns the salad into something closer to a Levantine tabbouleh; canonical Persian shirazi has only the mint.

Out of summer, when the tomatoes are not up to standard, leave them out. A cucumber and red onion version with extra lime and mint reads as half the salad but holds up better than the same dish with a watery winter tomato.

Salad-e shirazi takes its name from Shiraz, the southern Iranian city known for its gardens, its poets, and its lime trees. The salad is on every Iranian table at lunch and dinner, no matter the region. It is the everyday partner to the heavier rice dishes, kebabs, and stews of the Persian table.

The dish has cognates across the eastern Mediterranean, from Turkish çoban salatası to Greek horiatiki to Levantine fattoush. What sets the Persian version apart is the dice; smaller, finer, more uniform, and dressed with lime rather than vinegar. A bowl of shirazi and a plate of saffron rice is the canonical summer lunch.
